name: dumbbell one arm triceps extension (on bench)
instructions: Sit on a bench with your back straight and feet flat on the ground.
Hold a dumbbell in one hand and place your other hand on the bench for support.
Raise the dumbbell overhead, keeping your upper arm close to your head and your elbow pointing forward.
Lower the dumbbell behind your head by bending your elbow, keeping your upper arm stationary.
Extend your arm back up to the starting position, fully straightening your elbow.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then switch arms.

Sit on a bench with your back straight and feet flat on the ground. Hold a dumbbell in one hand and place your other hand on the bench for support. Raise the dumbbell overhead, keeping your upper arm close to your head and your elbow pointing forward. Lower the dumbbell behind your head by bending your elbow, keeping your upper arm stationary. Extend your arm back up to the starting position, fully straightening your elbow. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then switch arms.

conditionNotes.note: Keep load symmetrical and stop at positions that create sharp pain or twisting.
Use pain-free range of motion, slower tempo, and lighter progressions when joints feel irritated.
Weight-bearing resistance exercises are beneficial for bone density when load is appropriate.
Maintain neutral spine and avoid positions that aggravate the lower back.
Maintain consistent breathing throughout. Avoid breath-holding.
Both aerobic and resistance training improve insulin sensitivity. Monitor blood sugar before, during, and after sessions. Have a fast-acting carb available.
Rhythm-based exercises and large-amplitude movements are beneficial. Focus on motor confidence.
Focus on muscle co-contraction and joint stability. Avoid locking out joints passively.
Avoid supine positions after the first trimester due to vena cava compression. Incline or side-lie alternatives preferred.
Start light with higher reps and shorter ROM. Progress load gradually as pain-free capacity increases.
